channel.title: NPR Topics: Race
channel.description: NPR stories on race and ethnicity and race's effects on politics, culture, society.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: How the 1874 Freedman's Bank collapse connects to economic disparities we see today
item.description: In Savings and Trust, historian Justene Hill Edwards tells the story of the Freedman's Bank, which was created for formerly enslaved people following the Civil War. Originally broadcast Nov. 7, 2024.
item.content:encoded: In Savings and Trust, historian Justene Hill Edwards tells the story of the Freedman's Bank, which was created for formerly enslaved people following the Civil War. Originally broadcast Nov. 7, 2024.
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: As the U.S. turns 250, this historian has blunt advice: 'America has to grow up'
item.description: In America, U.S.A., Princeton historian Eddie Glaude Jr. looks at the country through the lens of its previous anniversaries and centennials. "The divided soul of the nation is in full view," he says.
item.content:encoded:
In America, U.S.A., Princeton historian Eddie Glaude Jr. looks at the country through the lens of its previous anniversaries and centennials. "The divided soul of the nation is in full view," he says.

(Image credit: Lisa Lake)
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: 'Race Unwrapped' podcast explores art as protest
item.description: The new season of the 'Race Unwrapped' podcast looks at art as a force for social change. Host Michelle Tyrene Johnson talks to NPR's Adrian Florido about the intersection of storytelling and protest.
item.content:encoded: The new season of the 'Race Unwrapped' podcast looks at art as a force for social change. Host Michelle Tyrene Johnson talks to NPR's Adrian Florido about the intersection of storytelling and protest.
item.dc:creator: Adrian Florido

item.title: How a single decision made a century ago split a family in half by race
item.description: Pope Leo's Black family roots inspired journalist Susan Saulny to research her Creole great-uncle who moved to Chicago, became white and didn't return. She describes her journey to reunite her family.
item.content:encoded: Pope Leo's Black family roots inspired journalist Susan Saulny to research her Creole great-uncle who moved to Chicago, became white and didn't return. She describes her journey to reunite her family.
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: A legal scholar and 'Backtalker' defends critical race theory — a term she helped coin
item.description: Kimberlé Williams Crenshaw helped name two contested ideas in U.S. politics — intersectionality and critical race theory. Her memoir chronicles the personal and legal framework for her thinking.
item.content:encoded:
Kimberlé Williams Crenshaw helped name two contested ideas in U.S. politics — intersectionality and critical race theory. Her memoir chronicles the personal and legal framework for her thinking.

(Image credit: Carl Timpone)
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: Push to save boarding school of first Native American to get Western medical degree
item.description: A man in Kentucky wants to preserve the remnants of the boarding school where the first native American who got a Western medical degree once lived.
item.content:encoded: A man in Kentucky wants to preserve the remnants of the boarding school where the first native American who got a Western medical degree once lived.
item.dc:creator: Stan Ingold

item.title: Even when Arsenio Hall's show was a hit, 'everyone wanted it to be something else'
item.description: Hall's late-night show gave hip-hop a home on TV and helped propel Bill Clinton to the White House. "I wanted to do this show that didn't exist when I was a kid," he says. Hall's memoir is Arsenio.
item.content:encoded:
Hall's late-night show gave hip-hop a home on TV and helped propel Bill Clinton to the White House. "I wanted to do this show that didn't exist when I was a kid," he says. Hall's memoir is Arsenio.

(Image credit: Kevin Winter)
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: At the Legacy Museum, facing America's racist past is a path, not a punishment
item.description: "There is an America that is more free — where there's more equality, where there is more justice, where there is less bigotry — and I think it's waiting for us," says lawyer Bryan Stevenson.
item.content:encoded: "There is an America that is more free — where there's more equality, where there is more justice, where there is less bigotry — and I think it's waiting for us," says lawyer Bryan Stevenson.
item.dc:creator: Terry Gross

item.title: Cesar Chavez abused and raped women and girls, NYT investigation says
item.description: A New York Times investigation has revealed allegations that the late renowned labor leader abused girls and raped Dolores Huerta, his longtime organizing partner.
item.content:encoded:
A New York Times investigation has revealed allegations that the late renowned labor leader abused girls and raped Dolores Huerta, his longtime organizing partner.

(Image credit: George Brich)
item.dc:creator: Adrian Florido

item.title: This historian dug up the hidden history of 'amateur' blackface in America
item.description: In her new book, Darkology, historian Rhae Lynn Barnes writes about how blackface and minstrel shows became one of the most popular forms of entertainment in 19th- and 20th-century America.
item.content:encoded: In her new book, Darkology, historian Rhae Lynn Barnes writes about how blackface and minstrel shows became one of the most popular forms of entertainment in 19th- and 20th-century America.
item.dc:creator: Terry Gross
